content: The strange thing is our company bought the devices which crashed with this issue, but we can not reproduce this issue on that device. Redmi Note 10 (M2103K19C) Galaxy A22 (SM-A225F) Galaxy A10s (SM-A107F) Y02 (V2217) Galaxy A22 (SM-A225M) Camon 19 (TECNO CI6) Redmi 10C (220333QAG) Galaxy J2 Core (SM-J260Y) Galaxy A21s (SM-A217F) Galaxy S22 (SM-S901B) Galaxy J7 Max (SM-G615F) Galaxy A12 (SM-A127F)

> The strange thing is our company bought the devices which crashed with this issue, but we can not reproduce this issue on that device. Redmi Note 10 (M2103K19C) Galaxy A22 (SM-A225F) Galaxy A10s (SM-A107F) Y02 (V2217) Galaxy A22 (SM-A225M) Camon 19 (TECNO CI6) Redmi 10C (220333QAG) Galaxy J2 Core (SM-J260Y) Galaxy A21s (SM-A217F) Galaxy S22 (SM-S901B) Galaxy J7 Max (SM-G615F) Galaxy A12 (SM-A127F) I've had this issue 100% reproducible on devices (Chromebooks). After upgrading ChromeOS to a later version, the problem was resolved. So my guess is that it's not always reproducible on the device type, you need to have the same ChromeOS version too. I'm far from certain of what the problem actually is but I am sure that I've have had this problem go away by upgrading ChromeOS (this was around September 2022 so not in recent Flutter versions). I also have a customer that uses two types of Chromebooks, one of the device types cannot upgrade ChromeOS passed version 103.x and my app (built using Flutter 3.7) crashes on this device. The same app has worked without problems on this device type when the app was built using flutter 3.3. The same customer have another Chromebook device with ChromeOS 105.x and the latest app version works on that device. It feels like the latest version of my app have some kind of requirement on ChromeOS that the old version did not have. NOTE! I've also updated target sdk versions and other stuff between these app versions so I'm not sure that it's the flutter version that actually causes the troubles, it can be targetSdk or some other package related to the build process. I'm trying to figure out if there a dependency between Flutter version/target SDK and required ChromeOS versions but so far I haven't found any relation here.
It looks like the issue persists with Flutter 3.7.x. Anyone with flutter 3.10.x having the same issue?

> It looks like the issue persists with Flutter 3.7.x. Anyone with Flutter 3.10.x having the same issue?

I suspect you have not solved the issue the way you think you have.

On most Linux based operating systems shared libraries (in this case, it's `libflutter.so`) are loaded before the program even starts executing.  So whatever changes you made to your application code is irrelevant, it's very likely your application code isn't even getting executed.

If the shared library cannot be found, it's possible the library search path is not correct.  That's one possible solution.  However, it seems to only affect certain old Android operating systems running x86.  I suspect the fix for this issue is very core to Flutter as a framework and how the library is bundled together in the build artifacts.  I doubt you can solve it at the application layer, probably needs to be solved in Flutter itself.

> I suspect you have not solved the issue the way you think you have.
> 
> On most Linux based operating systems shared libraries (in this case, it's `libflutter.so`) are loaded before the program even starts executing. So whatever changes you made to your application code is irrelevant, it's very likely your application code isn't even getting executed.
> 
> If the shared library cannot be found, it's possible the library search path is not correct. That's one possible solution. However, it seems to only affect certain old Android operating systems running x86. I suspect the fix for this issue is very core to Flutter as a framework and how the library is bundled together in the build artifacts. I doubt you can solve it at the application layer, probably needs to be solved in Flutter itself.

Hey guys, Hope this helps you.

I have been using this website https://playground.proguard.com/ (got from proguard manual website https://www.guardsquare.com/manual/configuration/usage) to check my proguard rules. You need to upload your APK/Appbundle to it and paste your proguard rules there and then check if whichever package is causing you trouble is obfuscated or not.

In my case `com.yendoplan.openappfile` was causing that UnsatisfiedLinkError and after adding the correct rule to proguard, its not obfuscated anymore and the error is gone. Rule: `-keep class com.yendoplan.** { *; }`

![image](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/14304128/cb2d5a82-a17d-41d9-a1fd-83bb6a31c404)

Reading through this issue I found out that those packages that are most likely to access platform specific functionalities are more subject to that issue

Read here:

https://developer.android.com/build/shrink-code#keep-code

> However, some situations are difficult for R8 to analyze correctly and it might remove code your app actually needs. Some examples of when it might incorrectly remove code include:
> 
> When your app calls a method from the Java Native Interface (JNI)
> When your app looks up code at runtime (such as with reflection)
